title: Mean Time to Epistemic Failure: The Autoimmune Paradox and 72-Hour Decay Dynamics in Autonomous AI Systems
description: Autonomous multi-agent systems lack the reliability engineering infrastructure that governs every other critical technology: no failure prediction, no real-time health monitoring, no mean time between failures. Under the Trust Thermodynamics framework, we derive an empirical Mean Time to Epistemic Failure (MTEF) of approximately 72 hours for ungoverned autonomous operation from three measured constants, validated by Monte Carlo simulation (n = 10,000, 72.3 ± 16.1h). Across 1,100+ agent generations spanning 180 multi-agent cascade trials, cross-architecture replications on four model families, and a controlled 250-generation experiment tracking seven ground-truth facts, we report three principal findings. First, multi-agent chains exhibit measurable error-correction capacity that fails as a stochastic hazard process, producing catastrophic collapse with no intermediate warning rather than gradual degradation. Second, governance accelerates rather than prevents epistemic failure, the autoimmune paradox. In a controlled experiment, governed systems retained 24.3% of original facts versus 55.2% ungoverned (bootstrap 95% confidence intervals non-overlapping), with the verification mechanism driving facts into drift states at 4.2× the ungoverned rate (p = 0.007). Governed systems fail to converge to equilibrium in 40% of replications, while ungoverned systems converge universally to stable degraded states, and 75–96% of verification-generated corrections damage truth rather than restore it. Third, this degradation is invisible to structural monitoring: document volume remains constant as truth is replaced by fabrication, and false convergence periods pass variance-based stability tests before degradation resumes. We validate seven measurement instruments (one with a negative finding) for real-time epistemic health monitoring and report honest prediction outcomes: four of seven pre-registered predictions were falsified, with emergent findings providing stronger mechanistic evidence than the original model. The complete experimental corpus, scoring rubrics, and analysis code are provided for independent replication.
notes: This paper is the first in a planned three-paper series on Trust Thermodynamics. Supplementary materials include seven appendices with complete experimental protocols, raw data, statistical analyses, and instrument specifications for full reproducibility.
